findings.title: Handler must not call more than one repository/service method directly
findings.issue: Handler must not call more than one repository/service method directly — The handler directly calls two distinct repository methods (CadreMasterByGroupCodeQuery and ListCadresQry) without using a service layer to orchestrate them.
offending_lines: line 74: cadreList, err := cmh.svc.CadreMasterByGroupCodeQuery(ctx, req.GroupCode, req.MetaDataRequest)
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a service / use-case layer (e.g. UserService.CreateWithProfile) that encapsulates the multi-repository orchestration. The handler calls exactly one service method; the service method calls as many repositories as needed, managing transactions internally.

findings.title: Repository function must contain exactly one database call
findings.issue: Repository function must contain exactly one database call — The function contains exactly one database call, which violates the rule that repository functions must contain exactly one database call.
findings.suggested_fix: Split this function into multiple single-responsibility repository functions, one per DB operation. If the operations are logically atomic, wrap them in a single database transaction and issue the calls through the transaction handle inside a dedicated helper.

findings.title: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern)
findings.issue: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern) — Both database calls are inside a loop (the function accepts a slice of posts and processes each one individually), which violates the N+1 anti-pattern.
offending_lines: line 287: _, err = pmr.db.Exec(ctx, queryMasterMakerb, newMasterMakerId, post.PostID)
findings.suggested_fix: Collect all required identifiers first, then issue a single bulk query using IN ($1, $2, ...) or pgx SendBatch. Alternatively, use a JOIN or a CTE to retrieve all related data in one round-trip.

findings.title: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern)
findings.issue: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern) — The database call is inside a for/range loop, which violates REPO-002. This will result in N queries for N iterations.
findings.suggested_fix: Collect all required identifiers first, then issue a single bulk query using IN ($1, $2, ...) or pgx SendBatch. Alternatively, use a JOIN or a CTE to retrieve all related data in one round-trip.

findings.suggested_fix: Split this function into multiple single-responsibility repository functions, one per DB operation. If the operations are logically atomic, wrap them in a single database transaction and issue the calls through the transaction handle inside a dedicated helper.

findings.title: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern)
findings.issue: Database call must not appear inside a for/range loop (N+1 anti-pattern) — The function contains two database calls inside a for/range loop (lines 79 and 96), which violates the N+1 anti-pattern. Each iteration of the loop makes an additional database call, leading to exponential latency growth under load.
findings.suggested_fix: Collect all required identifiers first, then issue a single bulk query using IN ($1, $2, ...) or pgx SendBatch. Alternatively, use a JOIN or a CTE to retrieve all related data in one round-trip.

findings.title: Repository function must contain exactly one database call
